title: Assisting with Thoracentesis and Paracentesis
subtitle: The nurse assists with fluid-removal taps — verifying consent, positioning the patient, coaching stillness, and watching for pneumothorax after thoracentesis and hypotension after large-volume paracentesis.

patient.name: Thoracentesis / Paracentesis
patient.admitDate: The nurse assists the provider with a thoracentesis or paracentesis and monitors for complications.

nursesNote: The nurse assists the provider with a thoracentesis (pleural fluid removal) or paracentesis (ascitic fluid removal). Before either, the nurse confirms informed consent and performs a time-out, gathers supplies, and explains the procedure. For a thoracentesis the patient sits upright leaning forward over a bedside table with arms supported, and is coached to stay still and avoid coughing or deep breathing while the needle is in; the main complication is a pneumothorax, so respiratory status is watched and a chest X-ray is obtained afterward, and no more than about 1000-1500 mL is removed at once to avoid re-expansion pulmonary edema. For a paracentesis the patient empties the bladder first to prevent bladder puncture and sits upright or in high-Fowler's so fluid settles to the lower abdomen; abdominal girth and weight are measured before and after, and after large-volume removal the nurse watches for hypotension from fluid shifts (albumin may be ordered). Throughout, vital signs are monitored, specimens are labeled, the site is checked for leakage, and findings are documented.
providerOrders: Verify informed consent and perform a time-out before the procedure
Position the patient: upright over a table for thoracentesis; voided and upright for paracentesis
Coach the patient to stay still and not cough during needle insertion
Monitor vital signs and watch for pneumothorax or hypotension
Label specimens, check the site, and arrange a post-thoracentesis chest X-ray

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about these fluid-removal taps?

opts.text: Thoracentesis removes fluid from the pleural space
For thoracentesis the patient sits leaning forward over a table
The bladder is emptied before a paracentesis
The patient should cough and move during needle insertion
Consent and a time-out are verified before the procedure
Pneumothorax is a risk after thoracentesis
Sitting upright lets ascitic fluid settle for paracentesis access
Emptying the bladder lowers the risk of bladder puncture
Removing too much pleural fluid at once risks re-expansion edema
Staying still during insertion helps prevent injury
Large-volume paracentesis can cause hypotension from fluid shifts
Sudden shortness of breath after thoracentesis can be ignored

Apply a dressing, label specimens, and arrange a CXR
Removes fluid from the pleural space
Removes ascitic fluid from the peritoneal cavity
Patient sits leaning forward over a table
Empty the bladder before the procedure
Watch for a pneumothorax afterward

Order a chest X-ray after the procedure
Watch for hypotension after large-volume removal

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: After thoracentesis: sudden SOB, sharp chest pain, diminished breath sounds
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

Apply oxygen and position the patient upright
Notify the provider of a suspected pneumothorax
Prepare for a stat chest X-ray
